text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY — SURVEILLANCE VISIT NOTE

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Routine 18-month post-treatment surveillance for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, base of tongue.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Margaret Chen is a 53-year-old East Asian woman, never-smoker, who was diagnosed approximately 18 months ago with HPV-16-positive squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, stage III (T2N1M0 by AJCC 8th edition HPV-positive staging) following workup for persistent left-sided throat discomfort and a palpable left level II neck mass. She was treated with definitive concurrent cisplatin-based chemoradiation — 70 Gy in 35 fractions via IMRT to the primary and gross nodal disease, with concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m2 every 3 weeks. She received 2 of 3 planned cisplatin cycles; the third cycle was held due to transient grade 2 nephrotoxicity. Radiation was completed without interruption. She had significant toxicities during treatment including grade 3 mucositis and a 10.5% body weight loss, necessitating PEG tube placement for nutritional support during weeks 5–7 of treatment. Post-treatment restaging PET-CT at 12 weeks showed complete metabolic response at the primary site and the left level IIA node, and she has had no evidence of disease on subsequent CT and PET-CT surveillance.

She presents today for her 18-month follow-up. She reports that overall she is doing very well and considers herself essentially back to her baseline. She returned to full-time work as a high school music teacher approximately 6 weeks ago, which she is managing without difficulty. She denies any new throat discomfort, dysphagia, odynophagia, globus sensation, or hoarseness. She denies otalgia, new neck lumps, or any new swallowing complaints. Her PEG tube was removed approximately 10 months ago. She is tolerating a normal diet; she notes that very dry foods — crackers, dense bread — can still be somewhat uncomfortable to swallow, but she manages these without issue with extra fluids. She denies any aspiration events or episodes of choking.

Her primary ongoing symptom is xerostomia. She describes the dry mouth as mild to moderate on most days, present all day but not debilitating. She sips water frequently during the school day and uses Biotene oral spray and over-the-counter saliva substitutes. She notes the dryness is noticeably improved compared to 6 months ago. She also notes mild neck tightness bilaterally, consistent with radiation fibrosis, which has been stable. There is no new trismus, pain, or restricted mouth opening.

Regarding her hypothyroidism: she was started on levothyroxine 50 mcg daily approximately 6 months ago for a rising TSH in the context of post-radiation hypothyroidism. Her dose was uptitrated to 75 mcg approximately 3 months ago and she reports feeling considerably less fatigued and improved energy levels since the dose adjustment.

She denies any new systemic complaints — no weight loss, no fevers, no night sweats, no bone pain, no new neurologic symptoms. She is not taking any new medications.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: mild xerostomia (improving), mild bilateral neck tightness/fibrosis (stable), intermittent mild fatigue (much improved since levothyroxine uptitration). Mild high-frequency hearing changes noted on prior audiogram (stable per patient report; no new tinnitus, no worsening).
Negative: denies throat pain, dysphagia, odynophagia, new neck mass or swelling, otalgia, voice changes, hoarseness, nasal congestion or epistaxis, headache, diplopia, facial numbness, shortness of breath, cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, abdominal pain, nausea, weight loss, fevers, chills, or night sweats. Denies peripheral neuropathy symptoms (resolved). No new skin changes.

MEDICATIONS
- Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally once daily (for radiation-induced hypothyroidism)
- Biotene oral spray as needed
- Fluoride prescription toothpaste (daily use per dental oncology recommendation)
- Multivitamin daily
- Acetaminophen 500 mg as needed (uses infrequently, last dose several weeks ago)

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
- HPV-16-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, left base of tongue, T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th ed., HPV-positive), diagnosed approximately 18 months ago; treated with definitive concurrent cisplatin-IMRT; complete metabolic response on PET-CT at 12 weeks
- Post-radiation hypothyroidism, on levothyroxine
- Post-radiation xerostomia (grade 1, improving)
- Mild high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss (grade 1 ototoxicity), attributed to cisplatin
- Mild radiation-induced cervical fibrosis (grade 1)
- Mild cisplatin-associated peripheral neuropathy, fingertips — resolved

SOCIAL HISTORY
High school music teacher, recently returned to full-time work. Never-smoker. Minimal alcohol use (less than 1 drink per week). Married, lives with spouse. Adult children, supportive family. Enrolled in a cancer survivorship program and actively advocates for HPV vaccination at her school.

FAMILY HISTORY
No family history of head and neck cancer. No family history of thyroid disease. No other significant family cancer history per prior documentation.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Affect bright. She looks well compared to her nadir during treatment.
Vital signs: Weight 59 kg (up 1 kg from prior visit 6 months ago; 3 kg below pre-treatment baseline of 62 kg, but trending toward baseline).
Head and neck: No facial asymmetry. Mild bilateral anterior neck skin hyperpigmentation/telangiectasia consistent with prior radiation dermatitis — cosmetically subtle. Mild palpable subcutaneous fibrosis of the bilateral anterior and lateral neck skin, non-tender, stable in character. No trismus; mouth opens to approximately 40 mm (within normal limits). Oropharynx: mucosa mildly dry but intact. No visible lesion, ulceration, or mass at the left base of tongue or elsewhere in the oropharynx. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy performed at this visit: the left base of tongue shows smooth, healed mucosa without any mass, nodularity, or friability. No neovascularization or abnormal vascularity. The right base of tongue and vallecula appear normal. Epiglottis and supraglottis unremarkable. Vocal cord mobility symmetric and complete bilaterally. No pooling of secretions.
Cervical lymphatics: No palpable cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy bilaterally. The previously documented left level IIA lymph node is no longer palpable. No axillary lymphadenopathy.
Skin: No concerning skin lesions of the scalp, face, or neck.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No tongue deviation, shoulder weakness (cranial nerve XI), or facial asymmetry.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No residual peripheral neuropathy in the fingertips on directed examination.

LABORATORY RESULTS (RECENT)
- TSH: 1.9 mIU/L (normal range 0.4–4.5) — improved/normalized on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily
- Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal) — not formally checked today; last result 3 months ago was within normal limits
- Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL (baseline, stable)
- CBC: not repeated at this visit (unremarkable at 12-month surveillance visit)

IMAGING
Most recent PET-CT (from approximately 6 months ago, at 12-month post-treatment surveillance): no FDG-avid disease at the primary site, regional nodes, or distant sites. Post-radiation changes in the oropharynx and bilateral neck stable. No evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease. No new imaging obtained at this visit per our standard surveillance protocol; next scheduled cross-sectional imaging will be at the 24-month mark.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-16-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, left base of tongue, stage III (T2N1M0) — No evidence of disease, 18 months post-treatment.
Ms. Chen continues to have no clinical, endoscopic, or radiographic evidence of recurrent or residual disease. The 18-month post-treatment milestone is reassuring; HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC has a favorable natural history in never-smokers with complete metabolic response, and her trajectory remains excellent. On flexible nasopharyngoscopy today, the left base of tongue mucosa is fully healed with no concerning findings. No lymphadenopathy on examination. I explained to the patient that she has reached an important milestone in her surveillance and that her prognosis remains very good. She understands the importance of continued monitoring given the small but real possibility of late recurrence or second primary, particularly in the larynx or hypopharynx.
- Continue surveillance per head and neck oncology protocol: clinical examinations and flexible nasopharyngoscopy every 3 months through year 2 (she has two remaining quarterly visits before transitioning to every 6-month visits in year 3).
- CT neck with contrast (or PET-CT if any clinical concern) planned at 24 months post-treatment.
- Patient counseled to present promptly with any new neck mass, throat pain, dysphagia, hoarseness, or ear pain.

2. Post-radiation hypothyroidism — well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily.
TSH 1.9 mIU/L on current dose; this is appropriate. She reports significant improvement in energy and fatigue since the dose uptitration. Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Recheck TSH in 6 months at next surveillance visit.

3. Post-radiation xerostomia — grade 1, improving.
Patient managing well with Biotene spray, saliva substitutes, and frequent hydration. No pharmacologic intervention with pilocarpine or cevimeline at this time; would revisit if symptoms become more bothersome. Continue meticulous dental hygiene and fluoride toothpaste. Dental oncology follow-up per their schedule.

4. Post-radiation cervical fibrosis — grade 1, stable.
Patient performing neck stretching exercises as recommended by physical therapy. No functional limitation. No jaw tightening or trismus. Continue current management.

5. Grade 1 cisplatin-associated hearing loss — stable.
No progression of audiometric changes reported subjectively. Formal audiogram deferred at this visit; will obtain at 24-month surveillance visit if not already done.

6. Survivorship.
The patient was counseled extensively on long-term survivorship. She has re-engaged fully with her career, quality of life is excellent by her report, and ECOG performance status is 0 today. She remains active in HPV vaccination advocacy at her school community, which was acknowledged as a meaningful contribution. Ongoing enrollment in survivorship program. She was reminded to maintain regular dental care given radiation to the salivary glands and the small long-term risk of osteoradionecrosis should dental procedures be needed — she should always alert any dental provider about her radiation history.

Return to clinic in approximately 3 months for next surveillance visit.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Head and Neck Medical Oncology

Time spent: approximately 40 minutes, greater than 50% devoted to counseling and coordination of care.
